question_text: Two identical springs are set up in series (connected end-to-end) and then a 10 N force is applied to the free end. Each spring has a spring constant of 200 N/m. Calculate the total extension of the two-spring system and explain why springs in series extend more than a single spring with the same force applied.
correct_answer: Total extension is 0.1 m. Springs in series extend more because the effective spring constant is smaller (100 N/m), so the same force produces greater extension.
explanation: In series, springs share the load but each adds its own extension. Using 1/k_total = 1/200 + 1/200, we get k_total = 100 N/m. Then x = F/k = 10/100 = 0.1 m. A single spring with k = 200 N/m would extend only 0.05 m under the same 10 N force, showing that combining springs in series reduces stiffness and increases extension.
